content.problemStatement:
There is an NNN by MMM grid. A thief is trying to escape from a policeman. The thief is currently in cell (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) in the grid, while the policeman is in cell (a,b)(a,b)(a,b). Cells are numbered from 111 to NNN, top to bottom, and 111 to MMM, left to right. Both of them are at different cells initially.

Both of them take turns alternatively. First it is the thief's turn, then the police's, and so on. The thief needs to reach the cell (N,M)(N,M)(N,M) to escape. In his turn, the thief can move either right, or down, from his current cell. In other words, if his current cell is (i,j)(i,j)(i,j), then in one turn, he can either go to cell (i,j+1)(i,j+1)(i,j+1) or the cell (i+1,j)(i+1,j)(i+1,j). He can't stop, i.e., he needs to move during each of his turns. The policeman can move right, down, or (right +++ down) in one turn. In other words, in one turn, if his current cell is (i,j)(i,j)(i,j), then he can go to the cell (i,j+1)(i,j+1)(i,j+1), the cell (i+1,j)(i+1,j)(i+1,j) or the cell (i+1,j+1)(i+1,j+1)(i+1,j+1). It is not compulsory for the policeman to move every turn, i.e., he can choose to stay in his current cell in a particular turn of his. Neither of them are in cell (N,M)(N,M)(N,M) at the start.

The policeman catches the thief if he's in the same cell as the thief at any point of time, and he had reached that cell before the thief. If the thief reaches the cell (N,M)(N,M)(N,M) before the policeman, then he shall escape. Find if the thief shall escape, or the policeman shall catch him, if both of them move optimally, and if both of them have perfect knowledge of the other person's location at all times. Print **YES** if the thief shall escape, else print **NO** if the policeman shall catch him.

Input:
The first line contains an integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM, denoting the number of rows and columns in the grid, respectively.
The second line of each test case contains two space-separated integers xxx and yyy, denoting the coordinates of the cell that the thief is in, initially.
The third line of each test case contains two space-separated integers aaa and bbb, denoting the coordinates of the cell that the policeman is in, initially.
###Output:

For each test case print **YES** if the thief shall escape, else print **NO**, in a separate line.

You may print each character of each string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes" and "YES" will all be treated as identical).

###Constraints


1≤T≤1051 \le T \le 10^51≤T≤105
3≤N,M≤1053 \le N, M \le 10^53≤N,M≤105
1≤x,a≤N1 \le x, a \le N1≤x,a≤N
1≤y,b≤M1 \le y, b \le M1≤y,b≤M
Both of them are in different cells initially.
Neither of them are in cell (N,M)(N,M)(N,M) initially.
The thief needs to move during every turn of his. The policeman can choose not to move.
constraints: The thief and the policeman start in different cells and neither starts at (N, M).
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases.
For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and M – the number of rows and columns of the grid.
- The second line contains two integers x and y – the starting cell of the thief.
- The third line contains two integers a and b – the starting cell of the policeman.
All indices are 1‑based. The thief moves first.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the thief can escape with optimal play, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Thief needs 1 move, police needs 2 moves → thief escapes.\nTest case 2: Thief needs 1 move, police needs 1 move. Since the thief moves first, he reaches (3,3) before the police can move there → escape.\nTest case 3: Thief needs 6 moves, police needs only 2 moves → police can wait on the column and catch the thief.

solution.approach:
Let D_thief = (N - x) + (M - y) be the number of moves the thief needs to reach the escape cell – this is also the number of his turns because he must move every turn. The policeman can increase his row and column by at most one in a single turn (right, down or diagonal), therefore the minimum number of his turns to reach (N, M) is D_police = max(N - a, M - b) (Chebyshev distance). The thief moves first, so before his D_thief‑th move the policeman has performed at most D_thief‑1 moves. The policeman can catch the thief only if he can be in the same cell strictly earlier, i.e. if D_police ≤ D_thief‑1. Consequently the thief escapes iff D_police ≥ D_thief. The condition is checked independently for each test case – O(1) time.
Proof sketch: If D_police ≥ D_thief, the policeman cannot be ahead of the thief in both coordinates at any moment; the thief can always choose a path that avoids any cell where the policeman could have arrived earlier. If D_police ≤ D_thief‑1, the policeman can reach (N, M) (or an intermediate cell on every possible thief path) before the thief’s final move, guaranteeing a capture.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); M = int(next(it))
        x = int(next(it)); y = int(next(it))
        a = int(next(it)); b = int(next(it))
        thief = (N - x) + (M - y)
        police = max(N - a, M - b)
        out.append('YES' if police >= thief else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
